ground_truth.title: Turning a tinybox Green v2 into a Private AI Home Server
ground_truth.author: Owain Brennan
ground_truth.main_content: TL;DR: I turned a tinybox Green v2 into a private AI server, media hub, and home automation brain-all accessible anywhere via Tailscale, with as limited as possible cloud dependencies. I've been wanting a proper local AI box for a while, as well as a home server, so why not both in one. Not a hosted playground, not "GPU hours in the cloud", but a machine that sits on my own network in my house, runs my own models and doesn't leak anything to anyone unless I deliberately push it there. Through a series of weird events from running and then selling my data science agency SeerBI, I ended up having a "spare" tinybox Green v2 I could use as I wanted. Power Considerations: This is a machine that can pull real wattage. For now, I've kept things simple - two outlets, default power limits. UK Note: Our 230V supply and higher-rated domestic sockets give a bit more headroom. I bought and set up a simple TP Link Powerline adapter. BMC: One of the big advantages of tinybox over a normal supe'd up gaming PC is the BMC (Baseboard Management Controller). I updated the system, created myself a real user, and added it to sudo. SSH continued to say Permission denied. The router had happily taken the IP I was testing against. The tinybox was sitting there on a different address. Tailscale: I have been following Tailscale for a few years, so when I finally got an excuse to use them I was very excited. So everything goes over Tailscale. No ports exposed, and with Tailscale's admin dashboard I can see all of my services, network logs and add any family members to the service. The Docker Stack: Everything lives in a single Compose file under /srv/stack with persistent volumes under /srv - all using Docker. The AI Experience: With Ollama + Open WebUI running, the "AI" side of this starts to feel real. I talk to them through a nice interface. Everything stays on the box unless I deliberately send it out. Observability with Netdata: The bit that turned this from "cool toy" into "actual system" was adding Netdata and exposing it via Tailscale. It's one thing to know, in theory, that a certain model size will stress memory or CPU. Locking down SSH too early - Turning off password auth before keys were confirmed is a great way to practice using the BMC console. Overcomplicating Tailscale - I was thinking it was a lot more complex than it ended up being. Why Bother? Every token, every request, every bit of context lives on your box, on your network, under your control.

I have been following Tailscale for a few years, so when I finally got an excuse to use them I was very excited.
Every token, every request, every bit of context lives on your box, on your network, under your control.
